title: Ensure Cloud Object Storage bucket access is restricted by  using IAM and S3 access control

input_row.title: Ensure Cloud Object Storage bucket access is restricted by  using IAM and S3 access control
input_row.description: Access controls on the Cloud Object Storage buckets are governed via IBM Identity and Access Management (IAM). However, some permissions can also be granted (or restricted) via S3 access controls.
input_row.rationale: Assign IAM access roles for users and Service IDs against buckets, by using either the UI or the CLI to create policies. The S3 Access Control is performed using ACLs granted against buckets and objects.
input_row.audit: Reviewing assigned access using IAM Complete the following steps to review your assigned access in an account that you have been added to: 1. In the console, click Manage > Access (IAM), and select Users or Manage > Access (IAM), and select Service IDs, depending on which identity you want to review. 2. Select your name or the service ID. 3. Review the assigned access in the Access policies section. Reviewing S3 ACLs to buckets View the S3 ACLs via API or CLI. A typical API call is shown: curl -X "GET" "https://{endpoint}/{bucket-name}?acl" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer {token}" \
input_row.remediation: Granting Access to a COS Bucket using IAM Using the UI To create a new bucket-level policy: 1. Navigate to the Access IAM console from the Manage menu. 2. Select Users from the left navigation menu. 3. Select a user. 4. Select the Access Policies tab to view the user's existing policies, assign a new policy, or edit an existing policy. 5. Click Assign access to create a new policy. 6. Choose Assign access to resources. 7. First, select Cloud Object Storage from the services menu. 8. Then, select the appropriate service instance. Enter bucket in the Resource type field and the bucket name in the Resource ID field. 9. Select the wanted service access role. Selecting the lozenge with the number of actions show the actions available to the role. 10. Click Assign Using the CLI From a terminal run the following command: ibmcloud iam user-policy-create \ --roles \ --service-name cloud-object-storage \ --service-instance \ --resource-type bucket \ --resource To list existing policies: ibmcloud iam user-policies To edit an existing policy: ibmcloud iam user-policy-update \ --roles \ --service-name cloud-object-storage \ --service-instance \ --resource-type bucket \ --resource The same set of accesses can be assigned to a service id as well. Please follow the procedure documented in https://cloud.ibm.com/docs/cloud-object-storage?topic=cloud- object-storage-iam-bucket-permissions#iam-service-id Granting S3 ACLs to buckets Grant the S3 ACLs via API or CLI. A Typical API call is shown: curl -X "PUT" "https://{endpoint}/{bucket-name}?acl" \ -H "Authorization: Bearer {token}" \ -H "x-amz-acl: "
input_row.impact: Organizations can use Cloud Object Storage to store sensitive data that may need to be restricted. Therefore, creation of a new Cloud Object Storage bucket requires careful review and planning around access controls provided by IBM Cloud environment to ensure data access can be restricted to only those having valid business need.
input_row.default_value: By default, users or service ids which have Cloud Object Storage service role access to the Cloud Object Storage service instances will have access to the Cloud Object Storage buckets under them as well.

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Automated

**APPROACH DECISION:**
The control can be fully verified via IBM Cloud APIs and IBM Cloud CLI commands as it pertains to access controls of Cloud Object Storage buckets using IAM and S3 access control. Both IAM policies and S3 ACLs can be queried through appropriate API calls and CLI commands, enabling an automated verification process.

**PROGRAM NAME:** ibm_cos_bucket_access_control_restriction

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
1. **APIs/CLI Methods:**
   - Use the IBM Cloud IAM API to list the IAM policies assigned to users or service IDs.
   - Use the IBM Cloud CLI command `ibmcloud iam user-policies` to list existing IAM user policies.
   - Use the IBM Cloud S3 API to check the ACLs of a specific Cloud Object Storage bucket.

2. **CLI Commands:**
   - To list IAM user policies:
     ```bash
     ibmcloud iam user-policies --user <user-id>
     ```
   - To create or update a user policy (as part of the remediation check):
     ```bash
     ibmcloud iam user-policy-create --roles <role> --service-name cloud-object-storage --service-instance <service-instance> --resource-type bucket --resource <bucket-name>
     ```
   - To get the bucket ACL:
     ```bash
     curl -X "GET" "https://<endpoint>/<bucket-name>?acl" -H "Authorization: Bearer <token>"
     ```

3. **Specific Resources to Enumerate:**
   - Review all Cloud Object Storage buckets within an IBM Cloud account to validate IAM configurations and ACL settings.

4. **Configuration Properties/Settings to Validate:**
   - Verify that each Cloud Object Storage bucket has appropriate IAM policies associated with the required service IDs and users.
   - Verify that no public access (anonymous access) is granted in the S3 ACLs.

5. **Pass/Fail Criteria:**
   - **Pass:** All IAM policies grant restricted access per compliance standards, and no S3 bucket ACLs allow public access.
   - **Fail:** IAM policies do not adequately restrict access, or S3 bucket ACLs allow public access.

6. **Example:**
   - Use `ibmcloud iam user-policies` to check if the assigned policies follow the principle of least privilege, providing only the necessary permissions for users accessing the COS buckets.
step2_review:
**APPROACH VALIDATION:** AGREE

- The classification of the audit approach as Automated is correct. The control can indeed be verified using IBM Cloud APIs and IBM Cloud CLI commands. The IBM Cloud IAM API and IBM Cloud S3 API provide the necessary capabilities to automate the verification of IAM policies and S3 ACLs for Cloud Object Storage buckets.

**TECHNICAL ACCURACY:** 8

- The automation details are mostly technically correct. The use of IBM Cloud IAM API and IBM Cloud CLI commands to list IAM policies and check S3 ACLs is valid. However, the command `ibmcloud iam user-policies` is used to list user policies, which is correct, but the remediation command `ibmcloud iam user-policy-create` should be used with caution as it modifies policies rather than just checking them.
- The curl command for checking the bucket ACL is technically correct but requires proper authorization handling, which should be explicitly mentioned.
- The program name `ibm_cos_bucket_access_control_restriction` is descriptive but could be more specific by including terms like 'iam', 'acl', or 'access_control' to clarify the exact checks being performed.

**IMPROVEMENTS:**

- **Authorization Handling:** The curl command should include detailed instructions on obtaining and using the Bearer token securely.
- **Program Name:** Consider renaming the program to `ibm_cos_bucket_iam_acl_check` to better describe the specific checks being performed.
- **Additional Checks:** Consider including checks for bucket policies if applicable, as they can also affect access controls.
- **Documentation:** Provide detailed documentation on how to interpret the results of the CLI commands and API calls, especially for users unfamiliar with IAM policies and S3 ACLs.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

- The assessment is well-founded on IBM Cloud capabilities and security best practices. The use of APIs and CLI commands aligns with the automated approach, and the technical details are mostly accurate.

**FINAL RECOMMENDATION:**

- Modify: Update the program name to `ibm_cos_bucket_iam_acl_check` for clarity. Include detailed instructions for obtaining and using the Bearer token securely in the curl command. Consider adding checks for bucket policies if relevant to the control.

step3_final_decision.decision_text:
**FINAL APPROACH:** Automated

**JUSTIFICATION:** Both AI opinions agree that the control can be automated using IBM Cloud APIs and CLI commands. The control involves verifying IAM policies and S3 ACLs, which can be efficiently checked using the IBM Cloud IAM API and S3 API. The technical capabilities of these APIs allow for comprehensive automation of the verification process, making manual inspection unnecessary.

**CONFIDENCE:** HIGH

---

**PROGRAM NAME:** 
ibm_cos_bucket_iam_acl_check

**AUTOMATION DETAILS:**
- IBM Cloud Resource/Service: Cloud Object Storage, IAM Policies
- API/CLI Method: 
  - IBM Cloud CLI `ibmcloud iam user-policies` to list IAM user policies
  - IBM Cloud S3 API to check the ACLs of a specific Cloud Object Storage bucket
- Properties to Check: 
  - IAM policies assigned to users or service IDs
  - S3 ACLs for buckets
- Pass Criteria: 
  - IAM policies correctly restrict access according to defined roles
  - S3 ACLs do not grant excessive permissions
- Fail Criteria: 
  - IAM policies allow unauthorized access
  - S3 ACLs grant permissions beyond what is necessary

**DISAGREEMENTS RESOLVED:** There were no significant disagreements between the AI opinions. Both agreed on the automated approach, and the second opinion provided a suggestion for a more descriptive program name, which was adopted.

**SOURCE:** CIS_IBM_Cloud_Foundations_Benchmark_v1_1_0
